title: Timing diagram — your flip-flop captures D on each rising clock edge
setup:
# --- plot helper (hidden) — a timing diagram driven by d_flip_flop ---
# Steps a clock and a D input through the student's own d_flip_flop and plots
# three stacked square waves: Clock, D, and Q. Q should change ONLY on a rising
# clock edge. If d_flip_flop isn't ready, Q stays flat and says so.
def __steps(vals, base):
    pts = []
    for t, v in enumerate(vals):
        pts.append([t, base + v])
        pts.append([t + 1, base + v])
    return pts

def __wave():
    N = 8
    clk = [t % 2 for t in range(N)]          # 0,1,0,1,... rising edges on odd ticks
    d_in = [1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0]
    ff = globals().get("d_flip_flop")
    ok = ff is not None
    q = 0
    q_series = []
    for t in range(N):
        clk_prev = clk[t - 1] if t > 0 else 0
        if ok:
            try:
                q = ff(d_in[t], clk[t], clk_prev, q)
            except Exception:
                ok = False
                q = 0
            if q not in (0, 1):
                ok = False
                q = 0
        q_series.append(q if ok else 0)
    return [
        {"name": "Clock", "points": __steps(clk, 4), "highlight": False},
        {"name": "D (input)", "points": __steps(d_in, 2), "highlight": False},
        {"name": ("Q (your flip-flop)" if ok else "Q (write d_flip_flop to see it)"), "points": __steps(q_series, 0), "highlight": True},
    ]
